title: Topic 353: Why Wishlists Drive Purchases: The Psychology of Delayed Gratification
content:
# Topic 353: Why Wishlists Drive Purchases: The Psychology of Delayed Gratification

## ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the peculiar human behavior of wishlisting, a digital ritual that has captivated consumers and baffled economists. We explore the psychological underpinnings of this phenomenon, from the sweet agony of delayed gratification to the quasi-endowment effect, which transforms a mere digital bookmark into a cherished possession-in-waiting. Through a rigorous, albeit humorous, analysis of consumer behavior, we reveal how wishlists manipulate our minds, turning fleeting desires into eventual purchases. We examine real-world examples from the gaming industry, where wishlists have become a powerful marketing tool, and conclude that the act of wishlisting is not merely a passive act of saving for later, but an active, and often irrational, step towards consumption. This paper will forever change the way you look at that ever-growing list of games you swear you'll play one day.

## INTRODUCTION

In the grand theater of consumerism, where desires are born and credit cards weep, a curious new act has taken center stage: the wishlist. It is a digital purgatory for products, a place where they linger between the realms of “want” and “have.” For the modern consumer, particularly the avid gamer, the wishlist is a sacred space, a carefully curated collection of future joys and potential disappointments. It is a testament to our aspirations, a monument to our optimism, and, more often than not, a graveyard of forgotten dreams.

But what is it about these lists that holds such sway over us? Why do we spend hours meticulously adding items, only to let them languish for months, sometimes years, before finally succumbing to the siren song of the “Add to Cart” button? Is it a rational act of financial planning, a noble exercise in self-control? Or is it something more primal, a psychological quirk that retailers have cleverly learned to exploit?

This paper argues for the latter. We will embark on a journey into the deepest, darkest corners of the consumer psyche to uncover the truth behind the wishlist. We will explore the tantalizing dance of delayed gratification, the seductive allure of psychological ownership, and the social pressures that turn a private desire into a public declaration of intent. We will draw upon the wisdom of psychologists, the cunning of marketers, and the collective groans of gamers who have all fallen victim to the irresistible charm of the wishlist. So, grab your reading glasses, and prepare to have your own consumer habits laid bare. You may never look at your Steam wishlist the same way again.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

To understand the modern phenomenon of the digital wishlist, we must first journey back in time, to a simpler era of psychological experimentation. Our first stop is the hallowed halls of Stanford University in the 1960s, where the now-famous “Marshmallow Test” was conducted. In this experiment, children were presented with a single marshmallow and a choice: eat it now, or wait 15 minutes and receive a second marshmallow. The results were telling. The children who were able to delay gratification, to resist the immediate temptation for a greater future reward, went on to achieve greater success in life. They had higher test scores, better health, and were less likely to engage in problematic behaviors. While the study has its critics, and the methodology has been questioned, the core concept of delayed gratification remains a cornerstone of psychological research. The ability to control one’s impulses, to choose the long-term benefit over the short-term pleasure, is a key indicator of success and well-being.

But how does this relate to our beloved wishlists? At first glance, the wishlist appears to be a tool for delayed gratification. We add a game to our list, telling ourselves we will wait for a sale, or until we have more time to play it. We are, in essence, choosing the two marshmallows of a future discount over the single marshmallow of immediate purchase. However, recent research suggests a more complex and, dare we say, more interesting dynamic at play.

This brings us to the work of Groening, Wiggins, and Raoofpanah (2021) and their concept of the “quasi-endowment effect.” They argue that online wishlists create a “liminal space” between ownership and non-ownership. When we add an item to our wishlist, we begin to feel a sense of psychological ownership over it. It is not yet ours, but it is also not *not* ours. It exists in a state of potentiality, a Schrödinger’s cat of consumer goods. This feeling of ownership, however tenuous, increases our attachment to the product and, paradoxically, makes us more likely to purchase it in the future. The wishlist, therefore, is not just a tool for delaying gratification, but a mechanism for cultivating desire.

Furthermore, the social context of the wishlist plays a crucial role. A public wishlist, one that is visible to our friends and followers, becomes a part of our identity. The games on our list are no longer just things we want to play; they are a reflection of who we are, or at least who we want to be. This social dimension adds another layer of pressure to the purchase decision, turning a private desire into a public performance.

## ANALYSIS

The psychological mechanisms that drive us to create and, eventually, purchase from our wishlists are a fascinating cocktail of cognitive biases and emotional responses. At the heart of it all lies the delicate interplay between delayed gratification and the quasi-endowment effect. Let us dissect this relationship with the precision of a surgeon and the wit of a stand-up comedian.

As we established in our literature review, delayed gratification is the grown-up version of not eating the marshmallow. It is the responsible, mature thing to do. And for a while, the wishlist allows us to feel like responsible, mature adults. We see a new game, a shiny, expensive new game, and instead of impulsively clicking “Buy Now,” we wisely add it to our wishlist. We pat ourselves on the back for our prudence, our financial acumen. We are the masters of our own desires.

But then, something insidious begins to happen. The quasi-endowment effect, that sneaky little gremlin of the consumer psyche, starts to whisper in our ear. “That game on your wishlist,” it says, “it’s practically yours already. You’ve chosen it, you’ve claimed it. It’s just waiting for you to bring it home.” And so, the game that was once a distant desire begins to feel like a cherished possession-in-waiting. We check on it, we read reviews, we watch gameplay videos. We are, in a sense, nurturing our desire, tending to it like a delicate flower. And the longer the game sits on our wishlist, the more it becomes a part of our mental landscape.

This is where the humor of the situation becomes apparent. We, the supposedly rational consumers, are being played by our own minds. The very act of delaying gratification, of putting the game on a wishlist, is what makes us want it more. It is a classic case of psychological jujitsu. The retailer, with a knowing wink, provides us with the tool to resist temptation, knowing full well that this tool will ultimately lead us to temptation’s doorstep.

And let us not forget the role of the sale. The sale is the catalyst, the spark that ignites the powder keg of our cultivated desire. When that game on our wishlist, the one we have been nurturing for months, suddenly goes on sale, it is not just a good deal; it is a sign from the heavens. It is the universe telling us that it is time to consummate our relationship with this digital product. The rational part of our brain, the part that was so proud of our initial self-control, is now screaming, “It’s 50% off! You’d be losing money if you *didn’t* buy it!” And so, with a triumphant click, we add the game to our cart, our journey from wishlist to ownership complete.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The gaming industry, with its digitally native products and its passionate, often impulsive, consumer base, provides a fertile ground for observing the power of the wishlist. The Steam Wishlist, in particular, stands as a monument to this phenomenon, a digital wailing wall where gamers confess their deepest desires and retailers reap the rewards.

Consider the case of the indie developer who, after months of toiling in obscurity, wakes up one morning to find that their game has been wishlisted by thousands of people. This is not just a vanity metric; it is a powerful signal to the Steam algorithm. The more wishlists a game is on, the more likely it is to be featured on the front page, to be included in sales, and to be recommended to other users. The wishlist, in this sense, becomes a self-fulfilling prophecy. The more people want a game, the more visible it becomes, and the more people will want it.

Then there is the psychological warfare of the Steam Sales. These seasonal events are a masterclass in consumer manipulation, and the wishlist is their primary weapon. Days before the sale begins, Steam sends out an email, a seemingly innocuous notification that “items on your wishlist are on sale!” This is not a gentle reminder; it is a call to arms. It is a carefully crafted trigger, designed to activate all the latent desire that has been simmering on your wishlist for months. The email creates a sense of urgency, a fear of missing out (FOMO) that is almost impossible to resist. You see that game, the one you’ve been eyeing for a year, now at a 75% discount, and your brain short-circuits. The rational part of you that was so proud of your self-control is now a gibbering mess, frantically entering your credit card details.

And what of the games that never leave the wishlist? The ones that are perpetually on sale, yet never purchased? These are the ghosts of our consumer past, the digital skeletons in our closet. They represent a failure of our aspirational selves, a reminder of the games we thought we wanted to be, but never became. They are the RPGs we never had time for, the strategy games that were too complex, the horror games that were too scary. And yet, we cannot bring ourselves to delete them from our wishlist. To do so would be to admit defeat, to acknowledge that we are not the gamers we once dreamed of being. And so, they remain, a silent testament to our ever-optimistic, ever-delusional consumer hearts.

## DISCUSSION

The implications of the wishlist phenomenon extend far beyond the realm of gaming. They offer a window into the very soul of the modern consumer, a creature caught between the conflicting desires for immediate gratification and long-term satisfaction. The wishlist is a microcosm of this struggle, a digital arena where our impulses and our aspirations do battle.

For marketers, the wishlist is a goldmine of data, a direct line into the desires of their customers. It allows them to track trends, to predict demand, and to tailor their promotions with surgical precision. The wishlist is not just a passive tool for consumers; it is an active tool for retailers, a way to cultivate desire and to guide the consumer journey from “want” to “have.”

For consumers, the wishlist is a double-edged sword. On the one hand, it can be a useful tool for financial planning, a way to keep track of desired items and to wait for a good price. On the other hand, it can be a dangerous trap, a mechanism for amplifying desire and for encouraging impulsive purchases. The key, it seems, is to be aware of the psychological forces at play. To understand that the act of wishlisting is not a neutral act, but one that is fraught with cognitive biases and emotional triggers.

Perhaps the most profound implication of the wishlist is what it reveals about our relationship with material possessions in the digital age. In a world where ownership is increasingly abstract, where we “own” licenses to digital files rather than physical objects, the wishlist provides a tangible sense of connection to the things we desire. It is a way of claiming a small piece of the digital world, of marking our territory in the vast and ever-expanding landscape of online consumerism.

## CONCLUSION

In the end, the wishlist is a mirror, reflecting our own complex and often contradictory nature. It is a testament to our capacity for both prudence and impulsivity, for both rational planning and irrational desire. It is a digital stage upon which we act out the timeless drama of human consumption.

So, the next time you find yourself browsing the digital aisles, your cursor hovering over the “Add to Wishlist” button, take a moment to pause and reflect. Ask yourself: are you truly delaying gratification, or are you simply cultivating a desire that will one day blossom into an inevitable purchase? Are you the master of your wishlist, or is your wishlist the master of you?

The answer, my friends, is probably a little bit of both. And that, in itself, is a truth worth adding to your intellectual wishlist. Now, if you’ll excuse me, I have a Steam sale to attend to. There’s a game on my wishlist that’s calling my name, and it would be rude to keep it waiting.

Groening, C., Wiggins, J., & Raoofpanah, I. (2021). Wish list thinking: The quasi-endowment effect’s impact on online wish lists outcomes. Journal of Consumer Behaviour, 20(2), 412–425. https://doi.org/10.1002/cb.1872
Mischel, W., Ebbesen, E. B., & Raskoff Zeiss, A. (1972). Cognitive and attentional mechanisms in delay of gratification. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 21(2), 204–218. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0032198
Psychology Today. (2017, December 26). The Benefits of Delaying Gratification. https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/your-emotional-meter/201712/the-benefits-delaying-gratification
